subject: Chemistry
title: Chelation: Removing Heavy Metals
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Chelation: Removing Heavy Metals.

hook.text: Chelation therapy uses molecules that grab toxic metals in your blood and drag them out through your kidneys.

buildup.text: Heavy metals like lead and mercury bind to proteins and won't leave on their own. The body can't excrete them.

discovery.text: Chelators like EDTA wrap around metal ions with multiple bonds—the word comes from Greek 'chele,' meaning claw.

twist.text: EDTA was first used in WWII to treat sailors exposed to lead-based paint. It saved lives within hours.

climax.text: Modern chelators are specific: DMSA grabs lead, BAL grabs arsenic, and penicillamine grabs copper.

punchline.text: Molecular claws grab toxic metals and drag them to the kidneys. Chemistry to the rescue.

quiz.question: What does the word 'chelation' mean?
options: Claw—chelators grip metal ions with multiple bonds like a claw
Chain—the molecules form chains around toxins
Clean—it comes from the Latin word for purify
